content_markdown: The gravitational field close to the surface of a planet is uniform. The gravitational potential difference between the surface of the planet and a point $30.0\text{ m}$ above is $15\text{ J\,kg}^{-1}$. What is the work done in raising a mass of $4.0\text{ kg}$ from the surface to a point $10.0\text{ m}$ above the surface?

diagram_metadata.description: No diagram provided in the image.

vibe_explanation: First, determine the gravitational field strength $g$. Since the field is uniform, $g = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta h} = \frac{15\text{ J\,kg}^{-1}}{30.0\text{ m}} = 0.5\text{ N\,kg}^{-1}$. The work done to raise a mass $m$ by a height $h$ is $W = mgh$. For a mass of $4.0\text{ kg}$ and a height of $10.0\text{ m}$, the work done is $W = 4.0 \times 0.5 \times 10.0 = 20\text{ J}$.
